instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
SEO Insertions on What are the Exclusions and Waiting Period under the Critical Illness Policy? - Article Page
Keyword density and no. of target keywords has been mentioned as comments in the beginning
Suggested additional anchor texts are marked as comments in Yellow. (If required)
Keywords: critical illness insurance, waiting period, critical illness plan, exclusions in insurance, critical illness cover
Body Content:
Introduction
Critical illness insurance plans have evolved from a good-to-have addition to your health insurance into an essential policy today. With the rising incidences of non-communicable diseases (NCDs), such as cancer, heart ailments, serious kidney disorders, etc., the need for insurance to cover the expenses of these treatments has also risen.
For instance, the cost of angioplasty can range from Rs. 2 lakhs to Rs. 4 lakhs, based on the type of stent you require and opt for. Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) are among the fastest growing chronic diseases in India today, with an annual growth rate of 9.5%. The cost of these treatments are high as well with healthcare inflation growing by leaps and bounds.
To avoid the high costs of treatment and related expenditure upon contracting such illnesses, it is best to opt for a critical illness plan early on in life. The premiums of a critical illness cover majorly depend on the age of the insured, which is why it is best to avail one when you are still young and relatively healthier.
However, there are certain clauses as well as terms and conditions associated with critical illness insurance plans that you must know of before you opt for one.
What is Waiting Period in a Critical Illness Insurance Policy
The waiting period of a critical illness plan depends upon the pre-existing disease or condition that a policyholder suffers from even before availing the plan. In most cases, the treatment costs for a pre-existing disease is not covered under a critical illness cover from Day 1 of the policy. The waiting period refers to the duration of time after which these diseases will also be covered under the critical illness insurance plan that the policyholder has availed. With most insurers, the waiting period is from the initiation of the policy till 90 days after it.
Major Terms and Conditions Under a Critical Illness Policy
While waiting period is one of the most major terms and conditions associated with a critical illness cover, there are several other terms and conditions you must check for before availing the policy.
Check how many diagnoses are covered by the critical illness insurance policy during the waiting period. For instance, several policies cover only the first diagnosis of kidney failure (end-stage renal failure), cancer, multiple sclerosis, and primary pulmonary arterial hypertension.
During the waiting period, many critical illness plans cover only the first instance of surgical procedures, such as aorta graft surgery, coronary artery bypass graft, major organ transplant, and heart valve surgery.
For many critical illness covers, only the first instances of total blindness, stroke, paralysis, coma, and myocardial infarction (heart attack) are covered during the waiting period.
Survival period is another clause covered within critical illness insurance plans. It refers to the insurer’s policy of paying claims for the disease diagnosed only if the patient survives for a minimum period of 30 days from the date of diagnosis. If in case the patient dies within that survival period, their nominees are not eligible to receive any compensation from the policy.
The length of waiting period and survival period can differ across insurers, so it is important to know your options by browsing through the available policies before making a decision on which critical illness plan you wish to opt for.
However, there are some standard exclusions followed by most insurers. Exclusions refer to certain causes of illness or disease, for which the cost of treatment will not be covered under the critical illness cover. Read on to find out the most common exclusions listed by insurers.
Self-inflicted injury or from suicide attempts are not covered.
Treatment for sexually transmitted diseases, such as HIV or AIDS, is not covered.
Injuries sustained by a policyholder due to participating in adventurous sports, such as parachuting or scuba diving, are not covered.
Cosmetic surgery costs are not covered.
Injuries caused under the influence of drugs or alcohol are not covered.
Conclusion
While the exclusions listed above are the most common exclusions followed by most insurers, it is important to go through the policy’s fine print to understand the exclusions in insurance. Waiting and survival periods are also essential details of your policy that you must know before you avail them.
